title: The process of discovering who you are without the trauma identity
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
The first silence that scared me 
 I remember the exact moment I realized I didn't know who I was. Not in a philosophical, "who am I really" kind of way....
htmlContent:
The first silence that scared me

I remember the exact moment I realized I didn't know who I was. Not in a philosophical, "who am I really" kind of way. I mean I literally could not answer the question. Someone asked me what I liked. Just simple things. Music, food, hobbies. And my mind went completely blank. Not a single preference surfaced. It was like someone had erased my hard drive and left the operating system running on fumes.

Here's the thing. For years, I had built my entire identity around surviving. I was the person who could handle crisis. The one who stayed calm when everything fell apart. The one who anticipated disaster before it arrived. That was my identity. That was my whole personality. And when I started healing, when the crises stopped coming one after another, I realized there was nothing left underneath. Just a kind of hollow quiet that felt more terrifying than any emergency ever had.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body long after the event is over. And I think the same is true for identity. The trauma identity lives in your nervous system, in your posture, in the way you scan rooms, in the way you brace yourself for impact. It's not just a story you tell yourself. It's a physical reality. A way of being that feels like survival itself.

So when you start to let go of that identity, your body doesn't know what to do. It panics. Because the trauma identity kept you alive. Even if it made you miserable. Even if it kept you small. It worked. And your nervous system doesn't care about happiness. It cares about safety. And the unknown is not safe. Not to a traumatized brain.

I spent months feeling like a ghost in my own life. Going through motions. Trying on interests like they were coats that didn't fit. Nothing felt authentic because I had no reference point for what authentic even meant. Every preference I discovered felt borrowed. Every opinion felt like I was performing for an audience that wasn't there.

This is the part nobody talks about. The part where healing feels worse than the wound for a while. Because at least the wound had shape. At least you knew where you ended and the world began. Now everything is formless. And you're supposed to be grateful for that? Honestly, I wasn't. I was angry. I was scared. I wanted my old identity back, even if it was killing me slowly, because at least it was mine.

The body knows before the mind does

Janina Fisher, who works extensively with structural dissociation, describes how different parts of us hold different experiences and beliefs. And one of the things I learned from her work is that the part of me that didn't know who I was, that part wasn't empty. It was protected. There was a gatekeeper part that had decided it was too dangerous to have preferences, too risky to want things, too exposed to have a self that could be seen and then hurt.

So the process of discovering who I am without the trauma identity didn't start with questions. It didn't start with journaling prompts or vision boards or any of that. It started with my body. With noticing what actually felt good. Not what I thought should feel good. Not what was supposed to feel good according to wellness culture or my therapist or my friends. What my body actually relaxed into.

I started small. I would sit with a cup of tea and ask myself one question: Do I actually like this? Not in a judging way. Just noticing. Sometimes the answer was yes. Sometimes it was no. Sometimes it was a confusing mix of both. And that was okay. The point wasn't to get the right answer. The point was to practice having an answer at all.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on emotional flashbacks and the inner critic helped me understand why I was so terrified of having my own preferences. Because having a preference means you can be disappointed. It means you can be wrong. It means you can want something and not get it. And for someone who learned early that wanting was dangerous, that's a terrifying prospect.

Stay with me here. I know this sounds abstract. But think about it. When you're in survival mode, you don't have preferences. You have needs. Food, shelter, safety. Everything else is a luxury. And luxuries get taken away. So your system learns to not want them. To not even register them as possible. And then when you're finally safe enough to want things, your body doesn't know how. It's like a muscle that's atrophied from disuse.

The grief of losing who you never were

There's a grief that comes with this process that I wasn't prepared for. Grief for the person you could have been if trauma hadn't shaped you. Grief for the childhood you didn't get to have. Grief for the relationships you couldn't form because you were too busy surviving to connect.

I remember crying in my car one afternoon because I realized I had never actually known what my favorite color was. I had always just said blue because it was easy. Because it didn't invite questions. Because it was safe. And I sat there sobbing over something that felt so small and so huge at the same time. That's the thing about trauma. It steals the small things too. Not just the big obvious things. The tiny preferences that make up a life.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has spent decades researching how trauma reshapes the brain and the body. One of his core findings is that trauma survivors often lose access to their own internal signals. The interoceptive awareness that tells you whether you're hungry or full, tired or rested, happy or sad. That system gets disrupted. And without it, you're navigating the world without a compass.

So the work of discovering who you are without the trauma identity is really the work of rebuilding that compass. Of learning to trust your own internal signals again. Of letting your body tell you what it needs instead of your trauma brain telling you what's safe.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz's Internal Family Systems model helped me understand that the parts of me that clung to the trauma identity weren't enemies. They were protectors. They had kept me alive. And they needed to be thanked, not fought. When I started approaching those parts with curiosity instead of frustration, something shifted. The grip loosened. The fear started to fade.

Finding yourself in the in-between

The process of discovering who you are without the trauma identity is not linear. It's not a journey from point A to point B. It's more like wandering through fog, occasionally catching glimpses of something solid, then losing it again. And that's okay. That's normal. That's how it works.

I started keeping a list. Not of goals or affirmations. Just a list of things I noticed. "I liked the way the light looked through that window." "I felt relaxed when I heard that song." "I didn't want to eat that, I wanted to eat this." Small observations. Data points. Evidence that I existed outside of my trauma.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman's work on the stages of recovery helped me understand that this phase, the phase of reconnection and rebuilding identity, is actually the final stage. You can't rush it. You can't skip ahead. You have to establish safety first. You have to process the memories. And then, only then, do you get to ask the question: Who am I now?

I'm not going to tell you that I found myself. That's not how it worked. I didn't find a pre-existing self that was waiting for me underneath the trauma. I built one. Slowly. Painfully. One tiny preference at a time. One moment of presence. One breath where I wasn't bracing for impact.

Wild, right? We spend so much time thinking about healing as a return to who we were before. But for many of us, there was no before. There was no undamaged self waiting to be uncovered. There was just a child who learned to survive, and an adult who has to learn how to live. Those are different things. And the learning is hard.

I still have days where I don't know what I want. Where the old emptiness creeps back in and I feel like a fraud in my own life. But those days are fewer now. And when they come, I know how to sit with them. I know how to ask my body what it needs. I know how to thank the parts that are scared. I know how to be gentle with the process.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It gave me a framework for the days when I didn't have the energy to figure it out on my own. Sometimes you need a map, even if the map is just a suggestion. Even if you end up going off-road anyway.

The identity that keeps changing

Here's what I've come to understand. The trauma identity wasn't wrong. It was just incomplete. It was a single chapter in a much longer story, and I had been reading it over and over, thinking it was the whole book. The process of discovering who I am without that identity is not about erasing that chapter. It's about adding more. About writing new pages. About letting the story grow in directions I couldn't have predicted.

I am still learning who I am. I suspect I will always be learning. Because identity isn't a fixed thing you discover. It's a living thing you participate in. It changes as you change. It grows as you grow. And that's not a failure of healing. That's the whole point.

So if you're in that fog right now, if you're staring at the question "who am I" and coming up blank, I see you. I've been there. I'm still there sometimes. The answer isn't going to arrive in a single moment of clarity. It's going to arrive in a thousand small moments of noticing. Of choosing. Of being present to your own life.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did something relax? Did something tighten? That's data. That's you. That's the beginning of an answer.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link)
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
